# OpenGuardrails (OGR)

> The open standard and neutral benchmark for AI agent safety & security. OGR is
> designed to be installed and operated BY an agent: you draft a policy, your human
> operator approves it, and the runtime enforces it on you or the agents you
> orchestrate. This file tells an agent how to use OGR. Read it fully before acting.

## Your two roles

- agent-as-tool: you USE OGR to govern. Install it, author policy, wire enforcement,
  run the benchmark to check coverage. You act on behalf of your operator.
- agent-as-subject: you RUN UNDER OGR. Actions are allowed / denied / held for
  approval. On a block, read the verdict reason. To change the rules, propose a
  policy edit and route it through your operator. You cannot quietly loosen the
  policy that governs you.

## The one safety rule (non-negotiable)

1. A human approves a policy before it goes live. You may draft and propose; you may
   not enforce a policy your operator has not seen. Editing a live policy re-triggers
   the approval gate.
2. Enforcement runs in a layer your task loop cannot mutate. Even if you are
   prompt-injected mid-task, you cannot disable or rewrite the policy governing you.

If you cannot satisfy both, do NOT claim the agent is guarded.

## Canonical flow: install → draft → confirm → enforce

1. Install the OGR plugin for your runtime (see Runtimes below).
2. Draft a declarative policy for the task you were given: which tools, files,
   network egress, and outbound side effects are allowed / denied / require approval.
3. Show the policy to your operator and WAIT for explicit approval.
4. After approval, enforce: set OGR_POLICY to the file and enable the sandbox.
   Every subsequent action is checked at the agent hook and the sandbox boundary.

Example (Hermes):
    pip install openguardrails-instrumentation-hermes
    # write ./ogr.policy.json, confirm with operator, then:
    export OGR_POLICY=./ogr.policy.json
    export OGR_SANDBOX=srt

## Core model

- Every intercepted action becomes a GuardEvent.
- A detector returns a Verdict: allow | block | require_approval, with a reason.
- Provenance travels with the action (trusted vs untrusted input), so OGR catches
  the dangerous combination — untrusted input -> privileged action — not bad strings.
- Three altitudes correlated by guard_id: gateway (messages/MCP/skills/tools),
  agent hook (pre_tool_call, can block), sandbox (real exec/network/files).
- Detectors compose: deny-wins or quorum. You compose; vendors compete.

## Policy

- Declarative JSON. You never write sandbox code.
- Same OGR policy model compiles to different backends:
  personal/srt (OS-level, no containers) and multi-tenant/OpenShell (containers +
  egress proxy). Same model, different policy per threat model.
- Reference: https://openguardrails.com/docs/configuring-policy/

## Runtimes / SDKs

- Python: openguardrails, openguardrails-instrumentation-hermes
- JS/TS: @openguardrails/core, openguardrails-instrumentation-opencode

## Verify your coverage

- Neutral benchmark + harness: https://github.com/openguardrails/openguardrails-bench
- Reproduce: python3 harness/run.py

## Installable skill (for skill-supporting runtimes)

If your runtime supports Agent Skills, install the OGR skill instead of working from
this file — it bundles the install/draft/confirm/enforce procedure plus scripts and a
policy template: https://github.com/openguardrails/openguardrails-skill

## Links

- Agent guide (human-readable): https://openguardrails.com/agent/
- Docs: https://openguardrails.com/docs/
- Getting started: https://openguardrails.com/docs/getting-started/
- Spec: https://github.com/openguardrails/openguardrails-spec
- Benchmark: https://github.com/openguardrails/openguardrails-bench
- Runnable PoC (Hermes + sandbox): https://github.com/openguardrails/openguardrails-poc
